facts.text: Disco evolved from funk, soul, and Philadelphia soul music in the early 1970s.
The word 'discothèque' originally referred to a French nightclub where recorded music was played instead of live bands.
The Bee Gees wrote the songs for Saturday Night Fever in just a few weeks while staying at the Château d'Hérouville in France.
The four-on-the-floor beat, a steady kick drum on every beat, became the rhythmic foundation of disco.
Donna Summer's 'Love to Love You Baby' featured 22 simulated moans and was initially banned on several radio stations.
Gloria Gaynor's 'I Will Survive' became an anthem of empowerment and is one of the best-known disco songs ever recorded.
KC and the Sunshine Band had five number-one singles on the Billboard Hot 100 between 1975 and 1979.
Studio 54 opened in Midtown Manhattan and became the most famous disco club in the world.
Chic's 'Le Freak' became the best-selling single in Atlantic Records history at the time of its release.
Giorgio Moroder is often called the 'Father of Disco' for pioneering electronic dance music production.
The Village People were created by French producers Jacques Morali and Henri Belolo to appeal to the gay disco audience.
Nile Rodgers co-founded Chic and went on to produce hits for Diana Ross, David Bowie, and Madonna.
The Saturday Night Fever soundtrack is one of the best-selling albums of all time, with over 40 million copies sold.
Disco balls, also known as mirror balls, were first used in nightclubs in the 1920s but became iconic during the disco era.
Bell-bottom pants became a staple of disco fashion, often paired with platform shoes and polyester shirts.
Roller disco combined roller skating with disco music and became a massive craze in the late 1970s.
Casablanca Records, founded by Neil Bogart, was home to Donna Summer, the Village People, and Parliament.
Philadelphia International Records, founded by Gamble and Huff, helped create the Philly soul sound that influenced disco.
ABBA won the Eurovision Song Contest in 1974 with 'Waterloo,' launching their international career.
Disco directly influenced the development of house music in Chicago during the early 1980s.
Larry Levan's residency at the Paradise Garage in New York helped define the genre of garage house.
Donna Summer's 'I Feel Love,' produced by Giorgio Moroder, was one of the first songs built entirely on synthesizers.
Frankie Knuckles, the 'Godfather of House Music,' got his start DJing disco records at the Warehouse in Chicago.
The Hustle was the most popular disco partner dance and was inspired by the Latin dance tradition.
Disco music typically has a tempo of 120 to 135 beats per minute.
The 12-inch single format was popularized during the disco era to give DJs longer mixes to play.
The Salsoul Orchestra blended Latin rhythms with disco orchestration and became hugely popular on dancefloors.
The Loft, run by David Mancuso in New York City, is considered one of the first modern dance clubs.
Cerrone's 'Supernature' fused disco with electronic and rock elements and was a massive European hit.
Disco Demolition Night took place at Comiskey Park in Chicago and is often cited as the symbolic end of disco.
Sylvester, known as the 'Queen of Disco,' was a pioneering openly gay Black artist in the genre.
The Trammps' 'Disco Inferno' gained renewed popularity after being featured on the Saturday Night Fever soundtrack.
Bee Gees members Barry, Robin, and Maurice Gibb were brothers born on the Isle of Man.
Tom Moulton, a remixer and producer, is credited with inventing the 12-inch disco single.
Barry Gibb's falsetto vocal style became one of the defining sounds of late-1970s disco.
John Travolta's white polyester suit from Saturday Night Fever sold at auction for $145,000 in 1995.
Studio 54 was known for its exclusive door policy managed by doorman Marc Benecke.
Bianca Jagger famously rode a white horse onto the dance floor at Studio 54 for her birthday party.
Donna Summer earned the nickname 'Queen of Disco' for her string of hits in the late 1970s.
Sylvester's 'You Make Me Feel (Mighty Real)' was one of the first hi-NRG disco tracks.
Van McCoy's 'The Hustle' reached number one on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1975.
Giorgio Moroder won three Academy Awards for Best Original Song and Best Original Score.
The Bee Gees wrote 'Stayin' Alive,' 'How Deep Is Your Love,' and 'Night Fever' for the Saturday Night Fever soundtrack.
Bernard Edwards played bass on many of Chic's biggest hits and was known for his melodic bass lines.
ABBA sold an estimated 385 million records worldwide, making them one of the best-selling artists ever.
The Bee Gees' 'Stayin' Alive' has a tempo of approximately 104 BPM, which matches the ideal rate for CPR chest compressions.
Platform shoes worn during the disco era could have soles as thick as four to six inches.
Sequined and metallic fabrics were popular in disco fashion, reflecting the lights of the dance floor.
Halston was one of the most prominent fashion designers associated with the disco era and Studio 54 culture.
After Disco Demolition Night, a riot broke out and the Chicago White Sox had to forfeit the second game of a doubleheader.
The anti-disco backlash led many radio stations and record labels to abruptly drop disco from their formats.
Andy Warhol, Bianca Jagger, Liza Minnelli, and Truman Capote were regular guests at Studio 54.
Despite the backlash, disco continued to thrive in underground clubs throughout the 1980s.
Post-disco emerged in the early 1980s as artists blended disco with electronic, funk, and new wave influences.
Nu-disco emerged in the early 2000s as a revival of disco aesthetics with modern production techniques.
The remix culture that dominates modern EDM has its roots in the disco era's 12-inch remix singles.
Walter Gibbons was one of the earliest disco DJs to create extended remixes for the dance floor.
Donna Summer's 'Hot Stuff' was one of the first disco songs to incorporate rock guitar elements.
Larry Levan's Paradise Garage in New York operated from 1977 to 1987 and influenced generations of DJs.
Disco pioneer Francis Grasso is credited with being one of the first DJs to beatmatch records.
The first commercially successful disco song is often considered to be 'Soul Makossa' by Manu Dibango, released in 1972.
Donna Summer's 'Last Dance' begins as a slow ballad before transitioning into an up-tempo disco track.
David Mancuso's Loft parties began on Valentine's Day 1970 and set the template for the club DJ experience.
Chic's Nile Rodgers and Bernard Edwards wrote 'Le Freak' after being turned away from Studio 54.
MFSB stood for 'Mother, Father, Sister, Brother' and was the house band for Philadelphia International Records.
Barry White's deep baritone voice and lush orchestral arrangements made him one of disco's most romantic artists.
The Andrea True Connection's 'More, More, More' was a disco hit sung by a former adult film actress.
A Taste of Honey's 'Boogie Oogie Oogie' won the Grammy for Best New Artist in 1979.
Alec R. Costandinos produced epic-length disco concept albums, including a disco adaptation of Romeo and Juliet.
ABBA's 'Dancing Queen' reached number one in over a dozen countries and remains their biggest hit.
Disco songs typically feature lush string arrangements, horn sections, and prominent bass lines.
Disco's influence can be heard in modern pop music by artists like Dua Lipa, The Weeknd, and Bruno Mars.
Dua Lipa's 'Future Nostalgia' album was a deliberate homage to disco and received widespread critical acclaim.
Saturday Night Fever starred John Travolta and was based on a 1976 New York Magazine article by Nik Cohn.
The term 'disc jockey' was shortened to 'DJ,' a term that became widely used during the disco era.
The disco backlash of the late 1970s was partly fueled by homophobia and racism, as the genre was rooted in Black and gay communities.
Kool and the Gang transitioned from jazz-funk to disco with hits like 'Get Down on It' and 'Celebration.'
Earth, Wind & Fire's 'September' is one of the most beloved songs associated with the disco era.
Diana Ross's 'I'm Coming Out' was produced by Nile Rodgers and Bernard Edwards of Chic.
Quincy Jones produced Michael Jackson's 'Off the Wall,' helping define the post-disco sound.
The Brothers Johnson's 'Stomp!' mixed funk and disco and was produced by Quincy Jones.
Daft Punk's 'Discovery' album drew heavily from disco, particularly the work of Nile Rodgers.
Rod Stewart's 'Da Ya Think I'm Sexy?' was a disco-influenced hit that angered many of his rock fans.
The Ethel Merman Disco Album, released in 1979, had the Broadway legend singing over disco beats.
Studio 54 co-owners Steve Rubell and Ian Schrager were convicted of tax evasion in 1980.
Cher's 'Take Me Home' album in 1979 saw her embrace the disco sound with great commercial success.
Barbra Streisand's 'The Main Event/Fight' was a disco hit that promoted her 1979 film The Main Event.
The Gallery, run by Nicky Siano, was another influential early New York disco club.
The Village People's 'Macho Man' was their breakout hit and established their campy, costumed image.
The Village People starred in the 1980 film Can't Stop the Music, which was a critical and commercial disaster.
Jacques Morali, co-creator of the Village People, was a French producer who moved to New York City.
Gloria Gaynor's 'Never Can Say Goodbye' is considered one of the first true disco hits, released in 1974.
The Bee Gees had been a pop group since the 1960s before reinventing themselves as disco artists in the mid-1970s.
Carl Douglas's 'Kung Fu Fighting' blended disco with novelty pop and sold over 11 million copies.
Robert Stigwood managed the Bee Gees and produced Saturday Night Fever and Grease.
Moroder's score for the film Midnight Express won the Academy Award for Best Original Score in 1979.
Donna Summer initially made her career in Germany, singing in the German production of the musical Hair.
Giorgio Moroder met Donna Summer in Munich, Germany, and their collaboration defined the Munich disco sound.
The Munich disco sound, also called Eurodisco, featured synthesizers and electronic production pioneered by Moroder.
The O'Jays' 'Love Train' is often cited as a proto-disco classic from the Philadelphia International stable.
Boney M. was a German-based Euro disco group created by producer Frank Farian.
Amanda Lear, a model and singer, had several Eurodisco hits produced by Anthony Monn in the late 1970s.
Cerrone, a French drummer and producer, released the album 'Love in C Minor' in 1976, which became a disco classic.
Alicia Bridges' 'I Love the Nightlife (Disco 'Round)' became a gay anthem and major disco hit in 1978.
Anita Ward's 'Ring My Bell' was a number-one disco hit in 1979 that was originally written for a younger singer.
Barry White's Love Unlimited Orchestra scored a number-one hit with 'Love's Theme' in 1974.
Disco Demolition Night was organized by Chicago DJ Steve Dahl, who invited fans to bring disco records to be blown up.
Amii Stewart's disco cover of 'Knock on Wood' reached number one in 1979.
The Sugarhill Gang sampled Chic's 'Good Times' bass line for 'Rapper's Delight,' one of the first mainstream hip-hop hits.
Sister Sledge's 'He's the Greatest Dancer' also topped the charts and showcased Chic's signature production.
Nile Rodgers produced David Bowie's 'Let's Dance' album, which became Bowie's best-selling record.
Silver Convention's 'Fly, Robin, Fly' won the Grammy for Best R&B Instrumental Performance in 1976.
David Bowie's 'Young Americans' was an earlier foray into disco and soul-influenced pop.
Grace Jones, a Jamaican-American model and singer, brought a post-punk edge to the disco scene.
The Paradise Garage was located on King Street in New York City's Hudson Square neighborhood.
Larry Levan was known for playing records on a custom-built Richard Long sound system at the Paradise Garage.
The Warehouse club in Chicago, where Frankie Knuckles DJed, gave its name to 'house' music.
The hi-hat pattern in disco, usually played in an open-closed sixteenth-note rhythm, became a signature element of the genre.
Ron Hardy, another Chicago DJ, played at the Music Box and was known for his raw, intense mixing style.
Bette Midler performed at the Continental Baths with her pianist Barry Manilow, who also became a star.
Barry Manilow's 'Copacabana (At the Copa)' was a disco-influenced pop hit that told a narrative story.
The disco ball works by reflecting light off hundreds of small mirror tiles glued to a rotating sphere.
Mirrored balls were first used in ballrooms in the 1920s, predating the disco era by decades.
Earth, Wind & Fire blended disco with funk, soul, and jazz, creating a unique and enduring sound.
Louisville-based company Omega National Products became the largest manufacturer of mirror balls in the United States.
Saturday Night Fever was set in the Bay Ridge neighborhood of Brooklyn, New York.
John Travolta trained for months with choreographer Lester Wilson to prepare for his dance scenes in Saturday Night Fever.
The Saturday Night Fever soundtrack spent 24 consecutive weeks at number one on the Billboard 200.
George McCrae's 'Rock Your Baby' was a number-one hit worldwide in 1974 and helped establish the disco sound.
The 'September' lyric '21st night of September' was chosen purely for its rhythmic feel rather than a specific event.
Thank God It's Friday (1978) was another disco-themed film that captured the nightlife culture of the era.
The 1980 film Xanadu, starring Olivia Newton-John, was a box office flop but its disco-influenced soundtrack was a hit.
Roller Boogie (1979), starring Linda Blair, was a film that capitalized on the roller disco craze.
The 1979 film Skatetown, U.S.A. featured roller disco and starred Scott Baio and Patrick Swayze.
Neil Bogart spent lavishly on promotion and was known for his extravagant lifestyle and parties.
Michael Jackson's 'Off the Wall' album blended disco with pop and R&B and sold over 20 million copies.
Gamble and Huff wrote and produced hits for the O'Jays, Harold Melvin & the Blue Notes, and Teddy Pendergrass.
Harold Melvin & the Blue Notes' 'The Love I Lost' is considered one of the earliest Philly disco records.
MFSB was a large collective of studio musicians at Philadelphia's Sigma Sound Studios.
Hues Corporation's 'Rock the Boat' is considered one of the earliest disco hits, reaching number one in 1974.
Thom Bell was another key Philadelphia producer who worked with the Spinners and the Stylistics.
Blondie's 'Heart of Glass' was a crossover hit that brought a new wave sensibility to disco.
The Spinners' 'The Rubberband Man' combined disco with Philly soul and reached number two on the charts.
First Choice's 'Armed and Extremely Dangerous' was an early Philadelphia disco hit from 1973.
Vince Montana Jr. led the Salsoul Orchestra and was a key figure in the Philly disco scene.
The Salsoul label released many classic 12-inch singles that defined the sound of the disco era.
Prelude Records was another important disco label, home to D-Train and Musique.
KISS released 'I Was Made for Lovin' You' in 1979, blending hard rock with a disco beat.
West End Records released seminal disco and early house tracks, including Taana Gardner's 'Heartbeat.'
Musique's 'In the Bush' was a raunchy disco hit that featured the vocals of Jocelyn Brown.
The film Thank God It's Friday won the Academy Award for Best Original Song with Donna Summer's 'Last Dance.'
Evelyn 'Champagne' King's 'Shame' was one of the biggest disco hits of 1978 and she was just 17 years old.
Donna Summer's 'On the Radio' was a greatest hits compilation that became a best-seller in 1979.
The Village People's 'Y.M.C.A.' became one of the most recognizable songs in pop music history.
Donna Summer appeared on the cover of Newsweek magazine in late 1979, symbolizing disco's mainstream dominance.
The Gap Band blended funk and disco with hits like 'Outstanding' and 'You Dropped a Bomb on Me.'
Rick James's 'Super Freak' combined disco, funk, and punk and was later famously sampled by MC Hammer.
Instant Funk's 'I Got My Mind Made Up (You Can Get It Girl)' was a huge disco club hit in 1979.
High-energy (Hi-NRG) was a faster, more electronic offshoot of disco that thrived in gay clubs in the 1980s.
Each member of the Village People represented a different stereotype: cop, Native American, soldier, construction worker, cowboy, and leatherman.
Italo disco emerged in Italy in the early 1980s, blending disco with electronic synth-pop.
MFSB's 'TSOP (The Sound of Philadelphia)' was the first disco-influenced song to reach number one on the Hot 100.
Italo disco artists like Klein & MBO and Ryan Paris had European hits but were relatively unknown in America.
Giorgio Moroder's 'Chase,' the theme from Midnight Express, became a disco instrumental classic.
Irene Cara also performed the title song for the 1980 film Fame.
Gloria Gaynor's 'I Will Survive' was originally released as the B-side of 'Substitute.'
The Ritchie Family was a Philadelphia-based disco group known for the hit 'The Best Disco in Town.'
Tavares, a family group from Massachusetts, had disco hits including 'Heaven Must Be Missing an Angel.'
The Jacksons' 'Shake Your Body (Down to the Ground)' was a disco-funk crossover hit in 1979.
Quincy Jones's production on 'Off the Wall' helped bridge disco, pop, and R&B into a new sound.
The Isley Brothers' 'It's a Disco Night (Rock Don't Stop)' showed even established soul acts embracing disco.
Donna Summer was born LaDonna Adrian Gaines in Boston, Massachusetts.
Thelma Houston's 'Don't Leave Me This Way' won the Grammy for Best Female R&B Vocal Performance in 1977.
Vicki Sue Robinson's 'Turn the Beat Around' became a disco classic after its release in 1976.
Santa Esmeralda's disco version of 'Don't Let Me Be Misunderstood' was a major international hit.
Dance Fever was hosted by Deney Terrio, who had choreographed John Travolta for Saturday Night Fever.
The bump, the robot, and the electric slide were popular dance moves during the disco era.
Boney M.'s 'Rivers of Babylon' was one of the best-selling singles in European history.
Line dancing in a disco format became common, with the Bus Stop being one of the first popular line dances.
DJs in the disco era developed techniques like beatmatching, blending, and slip-cueing that became standard practice.
Disco DJs often used two turntables and a mixer to create seamless transitions between songs.
Larry Levan was known for building musical journeys at the Paradise Garage that lasted all night.
Tee Scott was the DJ at Zanzibar in Newark, New Jersey, and influenced the New Jersey club sound.
Patrick Hernandez's 'Born to Be Alive' was a massive disco hit in 1979 that sold over 20 million copies.
David Rodriguez was a pioneering DJ at the Paradise Garage alongside Larry Levan.
The 'Disco Sucks' movement gained momentum in 1979, largely among white rock music fans.
Some historians argue that the anti-disco movement had undertones of racism, sexism, and homophobia.
Many former disco artists continued to make music throughout the 1980s under different genre labels.
Chic released several albums in the 1980s that incorporated new wave and synth-pop elements.
Chic's 'Good Times' is one of the most sampled songs in music history, used by the Sugarhill Gang in 'Rapper's Delight.'
Madonna's early singles like 'Holiday' and 'Lucky Star' were heavily influenced by disco and post-disco.
Prince's early work blended disco, funk, rock, and pop into a style uniquely his own.
Paul Jabara wrote 'It's Raining Men' and was known as one of disco's most prolific songwriters.
Freestyle music, popular in New York and Miami in the 1980s, blended disco with electronic and Latin beats.
Diana Ross's disco album 'diana' (1980) included the hit 'Upside Down,' which reached number one.
Nile Rodgers survived cancer and continued to tour and produce music well into his seventies.
Luther Vandross began his career singing background vocals on disco recordings before becoming a solo star.
Central Line's 'Walking into Sunshine' was a British disco-funk hit that topped dance charts in 1981.
The British disco scene thrived in cities like London, Manchester, and Birmingham during the late 1970s.
Munich Machine was a studio project by Giorgio Moroder that released instrumental disco tracks.
Jean-Marc Cerrone sold over 30 million records and was one of the most successful European disco artists.
Grace Jones's 'Pull Up to the Bumper' fused disco, funk, and new wave into a unique sound.
Gino Soccio was a Canadian-Italian disco producer who had a hit with 'Dancer' in 1979.
Dan Hartman's 'Instant Replay' was a transatlantic disco hit in 1978.
Ashford & Simpson were a husband-wife songwriting duo who also had their own disco hits.
Lipps Inc.'s 'Funkytown' became a worldwide number-one hit in 1980 and is considered one of the last great disco singles.
Ashford & Simpson's 'Found a Cure' was a major disco hit in 1979.
The sound system at the Paradise Garage was so legendary that audiophiles still reference it today.
The Brothers Gibb wrote songs for other artists too, including Barbra Streisand's 'Woman in Love.'
The disco era saw the rise of independent record stores, particularly those specializing in 12-inch singles.
Record pools, where labels distributed promotional copies to DJs, became essential to disco's marketing.
The Bee Gees' 'More Than a Woman' was never released as a single in the US but was a massive hit.
Samantha Sang's 'Emotion,' written and produced by the Bee Gees, was a top-five hit in 1978.
The Continental Baths in New York City was a gay bathhouse that featured live disco performances and launched Bette Midler's career.
The Four Seasons' Frankie Valli successfully transitioned from doo-wop to disco in the late 1970s.
The Bee Gees wrote 'Islands in the Stream,' recorded by Kenny Rogers and Dolly Parton, using a disco-influenced rhythm.
The Rolling Stones released 'Miss You' in 1978, incorporating disco rhythms into their rock sound.
Donna Summer was the first artist to have three consecutive double albums reach the Billboard top five.
The cover of Donna Summer's 'Bad Girls' album featured her standing on a city street in a provocative pose.
Modern disco balls can contain over 1,000 individual mirror tiles.
Nik Cohn's article 'Tribal Rites of the New Saturday Night' was later revealed to be largely fabricated.
Despite its fictionalized origins, Saturday Night Fever captured the spirit of working-class disco culture.
The Bus Stop dance was one of the earliest and most popular disco line dances.
Spandex and Lycra became popular disco fabrics because they allowed freedom of movement on the dance floor.
Gold chains and medallions were popular disco accessories for men, often worn with open-collared shirts.
The famous light-up dance floor in Saturday Night Fever was designed by set decorator George DeTitta Jr.
Disco fashion also popularized jumpsuits, hot pants, and tube tops for women.
Barbra Streisand and Donna Summer's duet 'No More Tears (Enough Is Enough)' reached number one in 1979.
The afro hairstyle was widely popular during the disco era and symbolized Black pride and cultural identity.
Farrah Fawcett's feathered hairstyle was another iconic look of the late 1970s disco and pop culture.
Studio 54 had a famous man-in-the-moon decoration with a spoon that was widely understood as a drug reference.
Saturday Night Fever was rated R upon its initial release but was later re-edited to PG for a wider audience.
The VIP basement area of Studio 54 was notorious for excessive drug use and sexual activity.
Studio 54 later reopened under different management and eventually became a Broadway theater.
Disco spread globally, with scenes developing in cities like Paris, Munich, London, Montreal, Sydney, and Tokyo.
Montreal's disco scene was one of the most vibrant in Canada, centered around clubs in the city's downtown.
Le Jardin was one of the earliest celebrity disco clubs in New York City, predating Studio 54.
Roller disco rinks like the Empire Rollerdrome in Brooklyn became gathering places for music and skating culture.
The Bee Gees' 'Jive Talkin'' was their first major disco hit, reaching number one in 1975.
The Funhouse in New York, run by DJ Jellybean Benitez, was an important venue in the early 1980s.
Jellybean Benitez later became known for producing and remixing Madonna's early hits.
Better Days was a New York club where DJ Tee Scott helped develop the house music sound.
Area in New York was a post-disco club known for its art installations that changed every six weeks.
Neil Bogart died in 1982 at age 39, and Casablanca Records was subsequently absorbed by PolyGram.
The Saint in New York was a massive gay disco with a planetarium dome above the dance floor.
The Saint had a capacity of about 4,000 people and operated from 1980 to 1988 in the East Village.
An estimated 50,000 fans attended Disco Demolition Night, thousands more than the stadium could hold.
The explosion of disco records on the field at Comiskey Park caused a riot that delayed the game.
Bee Gees records were among those destroyed at Disco Demolition Night, despite the group's enormous popularity.
Teddy Pendergrass was one of the first Black male artists to have five consecutive platinum albums.
After Disco Demolition Night, disco fell rapidly from mainstream favor in the United States.
Kylie Minogue's later career drew heavily from disco, with albums like 'Fever' and its hit 'Can't Get You Out of My Head.'
Jamiroquai blended disco, funk, and acid jazz, with hits like 'Cosmic Girl' and 'Virtual Insanity.'
Basement Jaxx, a British duo, incorporated disco elements into their electronic dance music.
Hercules and Love Affair's 'Blind,' featuring Antony Hegarty, was a critically acclaimed nu-disco track.
Sigma Sound Studios in Philadelphia was the birthplace of many iconic disco and soul recordings.
Scissor Sisters blended disco with glam rock and became one of the best-selling British bands of the 2000s.
Daft Punk's 'Random Access Memories' album was a love letter to disco, featuring Nile Rodgers on 'Get Lucky.'
Mark Ronson's 'Uptown Funk' featuring Bruno Mars drew heavily from disco and funk traditions.
The Weeknd's 'Blinding Lights' incorporated retro disco and synth-pop sounds into modern R&B.
Sister Sledge's 'We Are Family' was written and produced by Nile Rodgers and Bernard Edwards.
The Three Degrees were a Philadelphia girl group who had disco success with 'When Will I See You Again.'
Dua Lipa's 'Don't Start Now' was a disco-influenced hit that topped charts worldwide in 2020.
Lady Gaga's 'Born This Way' album contained several disco-influenced tracks.
Roisin Murphy has been a leading figure in nu-disco and electronic dance music since the 2000s.
Robyn's 'Dancing on My Own' is considered a modern disco-influenced classic of heartbreak pop.
Purple Disco Machine, a German DJ and producer, has been one of the biggest names in nu-disco since the 2010s.
Double Exposure's 'Ten Percent' on Salsoul is considered the first commercially available 12-inch disco single.
Dimitri from Paris is an acclaimed French DJ and producer known for his disco remixes and edits.
DJ Harvey is a British DJ who has been instrumental in keeping the disco tradition alive since the 1990s.
Horse Meat Disco, a London-based collective, has run one of the world's most respected disco parties since 2003.
Frankie Knuckles began DJing at the Warehouse in Chicago in 1977, where house music was born.
The Salsoul label's catalog has been extensively sampled by house and hip-hop producers.
Jocelyn Brown later became one of the most in-demand vocalists in dance music history.
First Choice's 'Let No Man Put Asunder' became a foundation track for Chicago house DJs.
Loose Joints' 'Is It All Over My Face?' bridged disco and the emerging New York underground dance scene.
Arthur Russell was an experimental musician who blended disco with avant-garde composition.
Gwen Guthrie's 'Ain't Nothin' Goin' On but the Rent' was a post-disco classic from 1986.
Colonel Abrams' 'Trapped' in 1985 was a post-disco house music crossover hit.
Evelyn King was discovered while she was cleaning the ladies' room at Philadelphia International Records.
The Pet Shop Boys blended disco, synth-pop, and Hi-NRG throughout their career.
Mel & Kim's 'Showing Out' and 'Respectable' were Stock Aitken Waterman productions with disco influences.
Casablanca Records was founded by Neil Bogart in 1973 and became synonymous with disco and excess.
Stock Aitken Waterman dominated the late 1980s UK charts with a disco and Hi-NRG influenced pop sound.
Ottawan's 'D.I.S.C.O.' was a French Eurodisco hit that spelled out the word disco in its lyrics.
Funkadelic and Parliament, led by George Clinton, influenced disco with their funk and outrageous stage shows.
The Gibson Brothers were a Martinique-born group who had European disco hits like 'Cuba' and 'Que Sera Mi Vida.'
Claudja Barry's 'Boogie Woogie Dancin' Shoes' was a transatlantic disco hit in 1978.
Lime, a Canadian duo, had Eurodisco hits in the early 1980s including 'Babe, We're Gonna Love Tonite.'
D-Train's 'You're the One for Me' was a post-disco classic produced by Hubert Eaves III.
Imagination, a British trio, had disco-funk hits like 'Body Talk' and 'Just an Illusion.'
Bobby Orlando was a Hi-NRG producer who worked with the Pet Shop Boys early in their career.
The Michael Zager Band's 'Let's All Chant' was a 1978 disco hit used in countless films and TV shows.
Kenny Gamble and Leon Huff co-founded Philadelphia International Records in 1971 and created the Philly soul sound.
Heatwave's 'Boogie Nights' was a 1977 hit that captured the excitement of the disco dancefloor.
Hot Chocolate's 'Every 1's a Winner' was a disco-funk hit from 1978 by the British-West Indian group.
Odyssey's 'Native New Yorker' was a 1977 disco crossover hit with lush orchestral production.
Giorgio Moroder produced the soundtrack for the 1983 film Flashdance, including Irene Cara's 'What a Feeling.'
Suzi Lane's 'Ooh La La' was a Giorgio Moroder production that became a disco hit in 1979.
Teri DeSario and KC duetted on 'Yes, I'm Ready,' which reached number two in 1980.
Shalamar's 'The Second Time Around' was a post-disco hit featuring future solo star Jody Watley.
Cheryl Lynn was discovered on the TV show The Gong Show before launching her music career.
GQ's 'Disco Nights (Rock-Freak)' was one of the last disco songs to top the R&B chart before the backlash.
Tavares' 'More Than a Woman' was featured in Saturday Night Fever alongside the Bee Gees' version.
Salsoul Records was founded in 1974 and specialized in disco, becoming an iconic label in the genre.
Musique's 'Push Push in the Bush' was a controversial disco hit from 1978.
Inner Life's 'I'm Caught Up (In a One Night Love Affair)' was a Salsoul classic featuring Jocelyn Brown.
Crown Heights Affair's 'Galaxy of Love' was a disco-funk hit from Brooklyn.
Disco music influenced Latin music genres such as salsa, merengue, and Latin freestyle.
Peaches & Herb's 'Reunited' was a disco-era ballad that reached number one in 1979.
Celia Cruz recorded disco-influenced salsa tracks during the late 1970s disco boom.
Karen Young's 'Hot Shot' was a one-hit-wonder disco classic from 1978.
Patrick Cowley's 'Menergy' and 'Megatron Man' were pioneering Hi-NRG tracks from the early 1980s.
Miquel Brown's 'So Many Men, So Little Time' was a Hi-NRG hit that became a gay club staple.
Hazell Dean was one of the UK's most successful Hi-NRG artists with hits like 'Searchin'.'
Disco dancing competitions became popular in the late 1970s, with TV shows like Dance Fever airing nationally.
Bobby 'O' Orlando produced tracks for Divine and helped shape the Hi-NRG sound of the 1980s.
The term 'Hi-NRG' was popularized by DJ Ian Levine, who championed the sound at Heaven nightclub in London.
Disco helped popularize the concept of the DJ as a creative artist rather than simply a record player.
Light shows and laser effects became standard in disco clubs, adding a visual dimension to the experience.
Fog machines were introduced in disco clubs to enhance the visual effects of laser and light shows.
Freestyle dancing was just as important as partnered dancing in disco clubs.
The strobe light became synonymous with disco dance floors and created dramatic visual effects.
Tom Moulton pioneered the disco breakdown, an instrumental section that built tension on the dance floor.
The 'disco mix' or 'extended mix' typically stretched a three-minute song into a six-to-ten-minute version for clubs.
Jimmy Castor Bunch's 'It's Just Begun' was widely sampled in hip-hop but originated in the proto-disco era.
Donna Summer released 'Bad Girls' in 1979, which blended disco with rock and new wave elements.
DJ turntables during disco typically used Technics SL-1200 models, which became industry standard.
DJ Kool Herc, considered the father of hip-hop, originally played disco and funk records at his parties.
Afrika Bambaataa's electro-funk classic 'Planet Rock' drew from both disco and electronic music traditions.
Grandmaster Flash developed advanced turntable techniques partly from watching disco DJs.
The disco era helped establish Black-owned record labels and gave opportunities to Black and Latino artists.
Many disco songs featured themes of love, celebration, freedom, and escapism.
Disco's emphasis on continuous mixed music led to the development of the DJ mix album format.
Songs like the O'Jays' 'For the Love of Money' blended social commentary with disco rhythms.
Harold Melvin & the Blue Notes' 'Wake Up Everybody' carried a socially conscious message within a disco framework.
The Philadelphia sound used lush orchestral arrangements, crisp rhythm sections, and soulful vocals.
Irene Cara's 'What a Feeling' from Flashdance won the Academy Award for Best Original Song in 1984.
The hi-hat played in a sixteenth-note pattern became as essential to disco as the four-on-the-floor kick drum.
Record companies quickly abandoned disco after the backlash, even renaming it 'dance music' or 'R&B.'
String arrangements in disco were often written by arrangers like Larry Gold, Bobby Martin, and Don Renaldo.
The wah-wah guitar effect was frequently used in disco to add a funky rhythmic element.
Fender Rhodes electric piano was a common keyboard instrument heard in disco productions.
Moog synthesizers were introduced to disco production by Giorgio Moroder and others in the mid-1970s.
Disco productions often featured large ensembles including string quartets, horn sections, and multiple percussionists.
Nile Rodgers produced Madonna's 'Like a Virgin' album in 1984, which became a massive hit.
Many disco recordings were mixed to be played on large club sound systems rather than home stereos.
ABBA's Benny Andersson and Björn Ulvaeus were skilled pop songwriters who adapted their craft to the disco sound.
Michael Jackson's 'Don't Stop 'Til You Get Enough' was his first solo number-one hit as an adult artist.
ABBA's music was featured in the hit musical Mamma Mia!, which debuted on London's West End in 1999.
The Mamma Mia! film starring Meryl Streep grossed over $600 million worldwide.
The Weather Girls' 'It's Raining Men' was a post-disco hit that became a gay anthem.
KC and the Sunshine Band were from Miami, Florida, and helped define the Miami disco sound.
TK Records, based in Hialeah, Florida, was the label behind KC and the Sunshine Band and other disco artists.
Betty Wright's 'Where Is the Love' blended disco with Southern soul and became a dancefloor favorite.
Walter Murphy's 'A Fifth of Beethoven' proved that classical music could be successfully adapted to disco.
Change was an Italian-American disco/R&B group whose members included Luther Vandross early in his career.
Space's 'Magic Fly' was a French electronic disco instrumental that became a European hit in 1977.
Dan Hartman also produced 'Relight My Fire,' which was later covered by Take That.
Wild Cherry's 'Play That Funky Music' blended rock, funk, and disco and hit number one in 1976.
Chaka Khan's 'I'm Every Woman' was a disco anthem written by Ashford & Simpson.
Andy Gibb, younger brother of the Bee Gees, had three consecutive number-one disco-pop hits.
Frankie Valli's 'Grease' was the title track for the 1978 film and topped the charts.
The Technics SL-1200 turntable was first released in 1972 and is still revered by DJs today.
Donna Summer's 'She Works Hard for the Money' in 1983 was a post-disco hit about working-class women.
Shannon's 'Let the Music Play' in 1983 pioneered the freestyle genre, a direct descendant of disco.
London's Heaven nightclub, which opened in 1979, became one of the most famous gay disco venues in Europe.
Andy Gibb's 'Shadow Dancing' spent seven weeks at number one on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1978.
Yvonne Elliman's 'If I Can't Have You,' written by the Bee Gees, reached number one in 1978.
Donna Summer's 'Bad Girls' album spent five weeks at number one and produced multiple hit singles.
The BPM counter became an essential DJ tool during the disco era for matching tempos between records.
Disco influenced the design of modern nightclubs, with dedicated DJ booths, dance floors, and lighting rigs becoming standard.
The whistle was a common accessory at disco clubs, used by dancers to express excitement on the floor.
Donna Summer recorded over 30 studio albums during her career and remained active until her death in 2012.
Chic's 'I Want Your Love' featured an iconic guitar riff by Nile Rodgers that has been widely imitated.
The disco era saw the rise of the concept album in dance music, with full-length LP sides designed for continuous play.
Many disco artists released concept albums where one side of the vinyl was a continuous mix.
Disco helped popularize the use of headphones by DJs for cueing up the next record in the mix.
The phrase 'boogie down' became slang for dancing during the disco era and entered mainstream vocabulary.
Disco's four-on-the-floor beat pattern continues to be the most common rhythm in electronic dance music worldwide.
